title: Regulating Disinformation in a Self-Regulatory Environment at the  European Level?
author: Beaufort, Maren
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description: While the original intent of the European regulatory approach was to assess laws and policies  in relation to elections, the COVID-19 pandemic and the ensuing "infodemic" provided another  relevant trigger for disinformation-related policymaking, based on the self-reporting of the  platforms. Content analysis of all 57 so called “Covid-19 reports” of signatories of the  European Code of Practice on Disinformation revealed fundamental shortcomings in the  processes of current regulation as well as transparency problems in the reporting system itself,  showing that regulation, standardisation, and transparency must be considered differently in  our new communication order.
steps_raw: While the original intent of the European regulatory approach was to assess laws and policies  in relation to elections, the COVID-19 pandemic and the ensuing "infodemic" provided another  relevant trigger for disinformation-related policymaking, based on the self-reporting of the  platforms. Content analysis of all 57 so called “Covid-19 reports” of signatories of the  European Code of Practice on Disinformation revealed fundamental shortcomings in the  processes of current regulation as well as transparency problems in the reporting system itself,  showing that regulation, standardisation, and transparency must be considered differently in  our new communication order.
